message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Convalescent plasma (CP) as a passive source of neutralizing antibodies and immunomodulators is a century-old therapeutic option used for the management of viral diseases. We investigated its effectiveness for the treatment of COVID-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Open-label, parallel-arm, phase II, multicentre, randomized controlled trial.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Thirty-nine public and private hospitals across India.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Hospitalized, moderately ill confirmed COVID-19 patients (PaO2/FiO2: 200-300 or respiratory rate > 24/min and SpO2 ≤ 93% on room air).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Intervention</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Participants were randomized to either control (best standard of care (BSC)) or intervention (CP + BSC) arm. Two doses of 200 mL CP was transfused 24 hours apart in the intervention arm.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Main Outcome Measure</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Composite of progression to severe disease (PaO2/FiO2< 100) or all-cause mortality at 28 days post-enrolment.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Between 22
                    <jats:sup>nd</jats:sup>
                    April to 14
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    July 2020, 464 participants were enrolled; 235 and 229 in intervention and control arm, respectively. Composite primary outcome was achieved in 44 (18.7%) participants in the intervention arm and 41 (17.9%) in the control arm [aOR: 1.09; 95% CI: 0.67, 1.77]. Mortality was documented in 34 (13.6%) and 31 (14.6%) participants in intervention and control arm, respectively [aOR) 1.06 95% CI: −0.61 to 1.83].
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Interpretation</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>CP was not associated with reduction in mortality or progression to severe COVID-19. This trial has high generalizability and approximates real-life setting of CP therapy in settings with limited laboratory capacity. A priori measurement of neutralizing antibody titres in donors and participants may further clarify the role of CP in management of COVID-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Trial registration</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The trial was registered with Clinical Trial Registry of India (CTRI); CTRI/2020/04/024775.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Convalescent plasma in the management of moderate COVID-19 in India: An open-label parallel-arm phase II multicentre randomized controlled trial (PLACID Trial)

reference.article-title: Improved clinical symptoms and mortality among patients with severe or critical COVID-19 after convalescent plasma transfusion
